Having reviewed the repo: it's a Go GitHub CLI extension with tag-driven releases via `cli/gh-extension-precompile`. Creating a versioning plan aligned with that setup.

[REDACTED]

input.name: GitHub release versioning
input.overview: Adopt **Semantic Versioning** on Git tags as the single source of truth, aligned with your existing `v*` release workflow and both install paths (`gh extension install` and `go install`). Optional automation (changelog, release notes) layers on top without changing the core pattern.
input.plan:
# Version management pattern for `gh-sr`

## What you already have

- **Release pipeline**: [`.github/workflows/release.yml`](.github/workflows/release.yml) runs on pushes to tags matching `v*` and uses [`cli/gh-extension-precompile@v2`](https://github.com/cli/gh-extension-precompile) — the standard way to publish **prebuilt binaries** for GitHub CLI extensions.
- **Local builds**: [`Makefile`](Makefile) sets `main.version` via `-ldflags` using `git describe`, so developers see a meaningful version when building from a clone.

That is the right foundation: **releases are tag-driven**, not branch-driven.

## Recommended pattern: SemVer on tags + GitHub Releases

| Layer | Convention |
|--------|----------------|
| **Version format** | [Semantic Versioning 2.0.0](https://semver.org/): `MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH`, optional pre-release (`-rc.1`, `-beta.2`) and build metadata if needed. |
| **Git tags** | Prefix with `v` (Go and `gh` ecosystem convention): `v0.1.0`, `v1.0.0`, `v1.2.3-rc.1`. Your workflow already matches `v*`. |
| **GitHub Releases** | One release per tag; **assets** come from `gh-extension-precompile` (users install binaries from the release, not from cloning `main`). |
| **Pre-releases** | Tag `v1.0.0-rc.1`, mark the GitHub Release as **pre-release** if you want it excluded from “latest stable” semantics in the UI; `gh extension install --pin` still works for testers. |

### User-facing install paths (both tied to the same tags)

1. **GitHub CLI extension (primary)**  
   - Latest release: `gh extension install an-lee/gh-sr`  
   - Pin a version: `gh extension install an-lee/gh-sr --pin v1.2.0` (binary extensions require a **tag that has release assets**).  

2. **Go toolchain**  
   - `go install github.com/an-lee/gh-sr/cmd/gh-sr@v1.2.0` — uses the **same** `v*` tags as the module; keep tags and `go.mod` module path in sync (no `/v2` until you intentionally ship **module v2** with a breaking API for importers).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  commit[Commit on main]
  tag[Annotated tag vX.Y.Z]
  rel[GitHub Release + assets]
  users[Users: gh extension / go install]
  commit --> tag --> rel --> users
```

## Operational rules (team discipline)

1. **Only tag releases from the branch you trust for production** (usually `main`). Avoid moving tags once published; use a new patch (`v1.0.1`) for fixes.
2. **MAJOR** = breaking changes to **documented** CLI behavior or config that you expect users to rely on; **MINOR** = backward-compatible features; **PATCH** = fixes.
3. **Document breaking changes** in release notes when you bump MAJOR (and optionally in a `CHANGELOG.md` if you want a single history file — not required for the mechanics to work).

## Optional: reduce manual work

These are **optional**; the core pattern is “tag → CI → Release”.

- **Changelog**: Maintain `CHANGELOG.md` ([Keep a Changelog](https://keepachangelog.com/) style) updated at release time, or generate release notes from GitHub’s “generate release notes” UI.
- **Automation**: Tools like [release-please](https://github.com/googleapis/release-please) or [GoReleaser](https://goreleaser.com/) can create tags, notes, and assets — only worth it if release frequency or consistency becomes painful. Your current precompile action already solves the hardest part (multi-platform binaries).

## Small consistency check (not blocking the pattern)

[`cmd/gh-sr/main.go`](cmd/gh-sr/main.go) uses `var version = "dev"`. The Makefile injects it for local `make build`. Confirm that **release binaries** from `gh-extension-precompile` also embed the tag (many such actions set `-ldflags` from `GITHUB_REF_NAME`). If `gh sr version` shows `dev` on installed release builds, add the same `-ldflags` pattern the Makefile uses to the release workflow inputs supported by that action.

## Summary

**Best practice for this repo**: treat **`v* SemVer tags` as the only release identifier**, let **`release.yml` + `gh-extension-precompile`** produce GitHub Releases and binaries, document **`gh extension install … --pin vX.Y.Z`** and **`go install …@vX.Y.Z`** for users who need pinning, and add changelog or release automation only if manual releases become a bottleneck.

todos.content: After next tag, confirm `gh sr version` on release binary matches tag; adjust gh-extension-precompile / ldflags if it shows `dev`.
Optional: add one line to README under Install for `gh extension install ... --pin vX.Y.Z` and `go install ...@vX.Y.Z`.
Optional: adopt CHANGELOG.md or GitHub generated release notes for each tag.

content.text:
Confirming `go_build_options` can't add a second `-ldflags` after the package path. Adding a release build script (matching upstream `gh-extension-precompile`) that injects `-X main.version=$TAG`.

[REDACTED]

input.contents:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Build matrix for GitHub CLI extension releases (cli/gh-extension-precompile).
# Injects main.version so `gh sr version` matches the release tag.
set -euo pipefail

TAG="${1:?release tag required}"

platforms=(
	darwin-amd64
	darwin-arm64
	freebsd-386
	freebsd-amd64
	freebsd-arm64
	linux-386
	linux-amd64
	linux-arm
	linux-arm64
	windows-386
	windows-amd64
	windows-arm64
)

IFS=$'\n' read -d '' -r -a supported_platforms < <(go tool dist list) || true

mkdir -p dist

for p in "${platforms[@]}"; do
	goos="${p%-*}"
	goarch="${p#*-}"
	if [[ " ${supported_platforms[*]} " != *" ${goos}/${goarch} "* ]]; then
		echo "warning: skipping unsupported platform $p" >&2
		continue
	fi
	ext=""
	if [ "$goos" = "windows" ]; then
		ext=".exe"
	fi
	GOOS="$goos" GOARCH="$goarch" CGO_ENABLED="${CGO_ENABLED:-0}" \
		go build -trimpath \
		-ldflags="-s -w -X main.version=${TAG}" \
		-o "dist/${p}${ext}" \
		./cmd/gh-sr
done

input.new_string:
- uses: cli/gh-extension-precompile@v2
        with:
          go_version_file: go.mod
          build_script_override: scripts/gh-extension-release-build.sh

input.contents:
# Changelog

All notable changes to this project are documented in this file.

The format is based on [Keep a Changelog](https://keepachangelog.com/en/1.1.0/),
and this project adheres to [Semantic Versioning](https://semver.org/spec/v2.0.0.html).

Release builds are tagged as `vMAJOR.MINOR.PATCH`. Each Git tag triggers a
[GitHub Release](https://github.com/an-lee/gh-sr/releases); the workflow also
generates release notes on publish. Use this changelog for a concise, curated
history when you want more than the auto-generated GitHub summary.

## [Unreleased]

### Added

### Changed

### Fixed

### Removed

input.description: Run vet and tests to ensure nothing broke

content.text:
Here is what was implemented:

### 1. Release binaries report the git tag (`verify-ldflags-release`)

`cli/gh-extension-precompile` always uses `-ldflags "-s -w"` and does not set `main.version`, so release builds would keep showing `dev`. A custom build script fixes that.

- Added [`scripts/gh-extension-release-build.sh`](scripts/gh-extension-release-build.sh): same platform matrix as the default upstream script (non-Android), with  
  `-ldflags "-s -w -X main.version=${TAG}"` and package `./cmd/gh-sr`.
- Updated [`.github/workflows/release.yml`](.github/workflows/release.yml) with  
  `build_script_override: scripts/gh-extension-release-build.sh`.

Validated locally: full script run + `./dist/linux-amd64 version` → `v1.0.0-test`.

### 2. README: pinning installs (`doc-install-pinning`)

Under **Install**, documented pinning for both flows and linked to GitHub Releases:

- `gh extension install an-lee/gh-sr --pin v1.2.0`
- `go install github.com/an-lee/gh-sr/cmd/gh-sr@v1.2.0`

### 3. Changelog (`changelog-optional`)

Added [`CHANGELOG.md`](CHANGELOG.md) in Keep a Changelog style with an `[Unreleased]` section and a short note that tags drive releases, GitHub generates notes, and this file is for a curated summary.

---

`make check` completed successfully. After you push and tag `v*`, CI will use the new script; `gh sr version` on installed release binaries should match the tag.
